text: BOLESKINE, a parish united with Abertarf, in Inverness-shire, stretching about 21 m. along the S side of Loch Ness, with an average width of 10 m. Pop. in 1801, 1,799; in 1841, 1,876. The v. of Fort Augustus is in this p.

BOLESLAW. See BUNZLAU. BOLESLAWICE, BOLESLAWIEC, or BUNZLAU, a town of Poland, in the circle of Kalisch, and 16 m. W of Wielun, and 6 m. SSE of Wieruszow, on the r. bank of the Prosna, on the Prussian frontier. Pop. 960.
BOLGARIO (MONTE), a summit in the ridge of the Neapolitan Apennines, which, running W, separates the bay of Salerno from that of Naples. Alt. 3,496 ft.
BOLETSH, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Servia, on the r. bank of the Danube, SE of Belgrade.

BOLGARY, or Ouspenskoi, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 60 m. S of Kazan, in the dist. and 15 SW of Spask, and about 5 m. from the l. bank of the Volga. Pop. 800. It contains numerous remains of the ancient town of the same name, the cap. of the Bolgares or Boulgares. Of these the most remarkable is a tower built of stone, 72 ft. in height.
BOLGHERI, a town of the grand duchy of Tuscany, in the prov. and 36 m. SSE of Pisa, and 9 m. SE of Cecina.
BOLHORST, a village of Westphalia, in the circle and 1 m. SW of Minden. Pop. 236. In the environs are extensive coal-mines. BOL

BOLI, a sanjak of Turkey, in Anatolia, bounded on the N by the Black sea; on the E by the sanjak of Kastamuni; on the S by Khudavendikar; and on the W by Kojahili.—Its cap., of the same name, stands in the centre of a plain, 16 m. in length, by 5 or 6 m. in breadth, 6 days' journey from Constantinople, on the banks of the Filiyas, called also the Boli-su. It contained about 1,000 houses in 1813, [Kinneir] and 800 Turkish houses, with a suburb of 40 Armenian families in 1830. [Dwight.] The ruins of the ancient Hadrianopolis are about 3 m. distant, on the N declivity of the Bithynian Olympus. Dwight and Smith, who crossed the plain of B. in May 1830, say that they found "no tract so fully cultivated in the whole extent of Asia Minor;" and Fraser beheld it, in April 1834, "one sheet of cultivation," covered even to the roots of the mountain-forests, with fields, enclosures, coppice, gardens, orchards, villages, and houses, "like England on a large scale."—About 4 m. SE of B., at a v. called Valajah, are mineral baths, which are much frequented.
BOLIES, a village in the p. of Duleek, in co. Meath. Pop. in 1831, 159.
BOLIKLEI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 140 m. SSW of Saratov, on the r. bank of the Volga, 45 m. SSW of Kamishin.

BOLILING, or BEILING, a town of the island of Bali, in the Asiatic archipelago, the cap. of a state of the same name, and, next to Karang-Assem, the most considerable town in the island. The state is said to contain 30,000 men capable of bearing arms.
BOLIMON, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, in the circle and 11 m. SSW of Sochaczew, on the l. bank of the Rawka. Pop. 950.

BOLINAO, a town and port of the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, on a peninsula of the same name, on the W coast, and to the W of the gulf of Lingayen, in N lat. 16° 20', and E long. 119° 50'.
BOLINALEA, a village in the p. of Rathnew, in co. Wicklow, near the Vartry river. Pop. 336.
BOLINGBROKE, a market-town and parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. W by S of Spilsby. Area of p. 2,570 acres. Pop. 919.—The St. John family take the title of viscount from this v.

BOLITZA, or Belitza, a river of Turkey, in the prov. of Albania, which takes its rise in the mountains to the NW of Pitzaro, and, after a tortuous course to the S of about 40 m., enters Lake Vivari, 9 m. SW of Delvino. Its principal affluent is the Pavla, which it receives on the r.

BOLIVAR, a county of the state of Mississippi, U. S., and on the river of that name. It comprises a superficies of 1,700 sq. m., and is drained by Sunflower river. Pop. in 1840, 1,356, of whom 971 were slaves. Its cap., Bolivar, is situated on the E bank of the Mississipi, and to the N of a lake of the same name.—Also a township of Alleghany co., U. S., in the state of New York, 15 m. E of Angelica, and 285 WSW of Albany. Its surface is hilly, and is watered by several small creeks. The soil is sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 408.—Also a town of Westmoreland co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 165 m. W of Harrisburg, on the S side of the Conemaugh. It consists of about 20 dwellings.—Also a village of Lawrence township, Tuscarawas co., in the state of Ohio, 8 m. N of New Philadelphia, on the Ohio canal, on the W side of the Tuscarawas.-Also a village of the state of Tennessee, cap. of Hardeman co., 162 m. SW of Nashville, near the S bank of the Big-Hatchee. Pop. about 500. —Also a village of the state of Missouri, cap. of Polk co., 132 m. SW of Jefferson city, 4 m. W of the Pomme de Terre. Pop. about 150.
BOLIVAR (Point), a narrow neck of land about 30 m. long, which divides East bay from the gulf of Mexico, on the coast of Texas.
BOLIVIA. A state of South America, sometimes known as Up-

PER PERU, lying between the parallels of 10° and 25° 40' S lat. According to what are understood to be the latest arrangements between Brazil, Peru, and Bolivia, the N boundary of the last state runs along the parallel of 10°, from the Rio Purus or Azara on the W, to the Madeira on the E; thus assigning to Bolivia a large tract of land lying between the Purus and the Rio Beni, which in most maps is represented as falling within the Peruvian frontier. From the point at which this boundary-line strikes the Madeira, at the junction of the Savare, the boundary-line runs up the Madeira, the Mamore, and the Guapore, to about the parallel of 13° where it leaves the l. bank of the latter river, and runs along the Sierra Guaravos or Melgueira, to the upper portion of the Paraguan river, along which it runs to nearly the parallel of 16°. It then runs in a general SSW direction along the Sierra Doirados or Fernando, till it strikes the Paraguay in about 20° 20' S, and runs up that river to the 22d parallel, at the junction of the Rio Galban. The boundary on the S, between La Plata and Bolivia, passes across the Gran Chaco, from the 1. bank of the Paraguay, along the parallel of 22°, till it touches the meridian of 62°, whence it runs in an irregular line, but gradually descending S, crossing the Cordillera del Desplobado, and running WSW till it strikes the great chain of the Andes in about 23° 10' S. It then runs along the Andes, skirting the E side of the great desert of Atacama, and descending from the mountains in about 25° 30' S, sweeps round to the shores of the Pacific, which it strikes at the head of Isla Blanca bay. From this point the coast-line of B. runs N to the mouth of the Rio de Loa, which forms the boundary, in this quarter, with Peru, in S lat. 21° 33', giving a length of coast to B. of 250 m. in a direct line. The present boundaries of B. on the W, with Peru, are the Rio de Loa to Guillagua; thence an irregular line running NE till it strikes the Andes; thence running along the Andes to Lake Titicaca; thence, after crossing the lake and assigning the W half to Peru, along the ridge whence the head-streams of the Beni descend, till it strikes the Amanto, a head-branch of the Purus; and thence down the Purus to the parallel of 10°. The boundary-line we have now traced assigns a very irregular outline to B. On the parallel of 12° its breadth from W to E is 320 m. Between the parallels of 16° and 21° it has a pretty nearly uniform breadth of 680 British m. Where it touches the coast, it is a comparatively narrow tongue of land; but during the various revolutions, with their corresponding re-distributions of territory, which have kept the political geography of the S American states in constant fluctuation, the coastline of B., and consequent breadth of territory, has been enlarged or contracted greatly according as the district of Arequipa has been attached to the fortunes of Peru or of B. Measured from N to S, on the meridian of 66°, the length of B. is 850 British m. Measured from its NE angle on the Madeira, to its SW angle on the Pacific, it is 1,150 British m.; and from its NW angle on the Purus, to its SE angle on the Paraguay, it has nearly the same length. Its superficial area is estimated in the Penny Cyclopædia [1836] at 350,000 sq. m.; in Macculloch's Geog. Dict. [1841] at 318,000 sq. m.; by Ungewitter [1845] at 14,600 German or 310,074 English sq. m.; by Berghaus [1843] at 27,040 German or 574,275 English sq. m. In the latter estimate, however, is included the prov. of Arequipa, Puno, and other districts, assigned to Peru in all the other estimates, and amounting to 132,100 sq. m., leaving 342,175 sq. m. to B. within the boundaries we have traced above. This estimate of Berghaus also comprises, we presume, the large district on the N lying between the Purus and the Rio Beni, which has probably not been taken into account in the other approximative estimates of surface. General features.] The western half of B. is occupied by prodigious mountain-ranges, and elevated table-lands, which stretch towards the centre of the country. On the extreme N, and all along the E and S frontier, we meet with vast plains,—here bare grassy steppes, there covered with majestic primeval forests, -through which flow the head-waters of the Amazon and the La Plata, themselves, in many instances, mighty streams. The Atacama, or coast-district, is a mere desert, sterile and featureless to the roots of the Andes as the Sahara itself. Mountains. The mountains of B. are a portion of the system of the Andes; and have been sufficiently described for general purposes in our article An-

DES, p. 250. The majestic peaks of the Sorata [alt. 25,400 ft.] and the Illimani [alt. 24,250 ft.] belong to the Andes of B. The sierra of Cochabamba and Santa Cruz, branching off from the eastern cordillera of the Andes, runs far to the E, till it gains a point nearly central to the whole region of B.; and the great bend of the Guapai river, or head-stream of the Mamore, marks the line where the mountain country on the W has sunk to the level of the great eastern plains, and of the Chaco on the S .- The great plateau, on which the lake of Titicaca reposes its vast expanse of water, at an alt. of 12,795 ft. above the level of the Pacific, may be regarded as belonging to the mountain-system of B., and as forming one of the most remarkable features of the country. It lies between the two chains of the B. Andes, and, with the exception of the great table-land of Tibet, forms the most elevated table-land in the world. The lake itself appears, in most maps, as divided between Peru and B.; but the Rio Desaguadero, which, flowing from its SE extremity, appears to convey its superfluous waters to the Lago del Desaguadero, or Lake Ullagas, in a deep but slow stream of 180 m. in length, belongs wholly to B. Rivers. The great intersecting rivers of B. are the Rio Beni, the Rio Mamore, the Rio Blanco or Ubai, and the Pilcomayo, with their numerous tributaries. All these great rivers, with the Paraguay and the Madeira on the boundaries, are navigable streams; and as such are destined to open up, by means of steam-navigation, the yet unexplored resources of this noble country. The chief upper branches of the Rio Beni are the Rio Caca or Mapiri, the Rio Chuqueapo, and the Rio Quetolo. The Mamore receives the Rio de Exaltacion, the Rio Santa Anna, the Apere, the Esecuri, the Sipuri, the Rio San José, the Chapari, all on the l. bank, and the Tapacures, on the r., below that part of its upper course in which it bears the name of the Guapai, and in which its principal tributaries are the Rio Cochabamba, the Rio Grande, the Rio Azero, and the Rio Flores. Between the Rio Blanco, and the basin of the Guapore, is the Baures, and the Paraguan, both large rivers, with numerous tributaries, flowing N by W to the Guapore. The chief branches of the Paraguay, rising in B., are the Rio San Juan or Tapera, the Rio la Cruz, and the Rio Bartolomeo. From May to August the valley of the Paraguay, where it borders on B., appears to be laid under water, from 70 to 150 m. in breadth; and this annual inundation has given occasion to the introduction in some maps of a large lake in this quarter, called the lake of Xarages. The chief tributary of the Pilcomayo is the Pilaya, and its head-stream, the San Juan. The rivers which originate on the W declivity of the Andes, and flow into the Pacific, in the Atacama district, are of trifling magnitude and no importance. Climate and Productions.] Rain seldom falls on the Atacama coast, which seems to be so scantily supplied with water that it can never exchange its present desert state for the clothing and verdure of cultivation. The great table-land of Titicaca has a mean temp. of 45°; and from November to April enjoys delightfully refreshing showers. Here the pasture is rich, and numerous herds of cattle and sheep are reared; but the tropical fruits are unknown; and wheat, rye, and barley, do not ripen. The temp. of the great plains on the N, E, and S frontiers of B., is most oppressive; and being combined with great humidity, these districts are often highly insalubrious. Here indigo, cotton, rice, mandioc, cocoa, oranges, pine-apples, and all the tropical fruits, grow freely. The derate climate, raise the grains and fruits of Europe, in conjunction with the productions of the tropics. Around Potosi, the temp. varies greatly throughout the day. Early in the morning it is cold and piercing; the forenoon resembles one of the finer days of March in England; from noon till 3 P. M. it is generally very hot in the sun; while the evenings are serene and mild. The forests of B. supply sarsaparilla, caoutchouc, vanilla, coca, and a variety of gums.—The animal kingdom embraces the different species of the llama family, known as llamas, guanacos, alpacas, and vicunas. These exist in great numbers in the lofty, dry, and arid cordilleras; and supply the inhabitants of these regions with their beasts of burden, as well as with wool and flesh. The tapir, the jaguar, and the leopard, roam in the forests; and the chinchilla supplies a valuable article of commerce in its fur. But the great wealth of B. has for ages been drawn from the bowels of the earth, especially from its gold and silver-mines. The silvermines of Potosi were once the richest in the world, though they no longer hold that distinction, their produce having gradually diminished since 1694. Copper is also abundant; and iron, lead, tin, saltpetre, limestone, and salt, occur in several places. A silver-mine of great richness was discovered, it is said, in 1846, at Corocoro in La Paz. Commerce. The commerce of B. is almost limited to the export of bullion and alpaca wool to Europe, and of grain, and coca—an article extensively used by the Indians—to Peru. An important traffic in cattle was at one time maintained between B. and the Rio de la Plata provinces: the latter furnishing cattle and mules, and the former paying in bullion and native produce. But this branch of commerce has been nearly annihilated by the continued political disturbances in both countries. Tacna and Arequipa were the principal ports through which B. received European produce before the war with Peru. An attempt has been made by the government to convert the port of Cobija, at the N extremity of the Atacama desert, into a port for the introduction of goods into B. so as to avoid the heavy transit duties, and other charges, to which they are subjected in the puertos intermedios of Peru. Under the name of El Puerto Lamar, Cobija was erected into a free port; but though all encouragement has been given to merchants to use this port by an almost entire exemption from duties, yet such is the arid and inhospitable nature of the country, and the difficulty of the routes leading into the interior from this point, that the place has not succeeded so well as was anticipated. In 1840, 92 vessels entered Cobija, of which 33 were English; 15 French; 3 Spanish; 4 N American; 4 Sardinian; 1 Mexican; 1 from Granada; 2 from | vice-president, and three secretaries-of-state. The BOLIVIA. Ecuador; 20 from Chili; and 15 Peruvian. The ships which anchored in the port in 1843, were 91, exclusive of steam-vessels, ships-of-war, and sailing packets. Of these 21 were English; and 24 Chilian. The exportation of money through this port in 1843, was 1,638,705 dollars, besides 91,828 dollars circulating in the district; and the importation of merchandise in the same year amounted to 1,534,822 dollars. The importation and exportation by the land-frontiers in 1840 were as follows:- Exports in bullion. Imports. 8,389 dollars. Cochabamba, 9,750 dollars. Oruro, The total exportation in merchandise amounted to the approximate value of 95,668 dollars, or £19,134. Among the principal articles of export were— 624 quintals value 8,743 dollars. Soap, 1,453 arrobas of 91 lbs. Tobacco, 10,013 quintals Bark, The money coined at the national mint of Potosi in 1840 amounted to 2,830,891 dollars <math>=</math> £566,178. Of this 2,600,507 dollars were in silver; and 230,384 dollars in gold. Population.] The pop. of B. is variously estimated at from 630,000 [Penny Cyclop. in 1836] to 2,400,000 [Berghaus, 1843]. The latter estimate, however, included Arequipa. Pöppig estimates it at 1,200,000; the Almanach de Gotha for 1849, at 1,030,000; while the Encyclop. Britannica offers a detailed approximation carrying the pop. to 1,716,000 several years ago. All these estimates are in great part conjectural. The greater part of the pop. are Indians. The native pop. of the coast and of the table-land of Titicaca, are Quichuas of Peruvian extraction. The Moxos and Chiquitos, in various tribes, are spread over the western part of the state. The Mestizoes and Creoles are nearly equal in number. Foreign settlers and inhabitants of purely Spanish descent are chiefly located in the mining districts.—The Spanish language is in general use in B.; but the Quichua is also spoken by the Quichua Indians; and the Amiaru is the language in common use in the dep. of La Paz. Government.] The constitution of B. as originally drawn up by Bolivar vested the supreme authority in a presidente vitalicio, that is, a president for life, with the power of naming his successors; and guaranteed equality of rights, a free press, and the free exercise of thoughts and opinions, professions and trades; while it annihilated all class privileges and employments, and traced all legitimate power directly to the people. Every 10 citizens nominate an elector, to exercise a delegated authority for four years. The parties thus commissioned elect from among themselves three legislative chambers of 30 members each. Those for the chamber-of-tribunes being nominated for 4 years, and renewed by moieties every 2; those for the senate, for 8 years; and those of the chamber-of-censors for life. The functions of the chamber-of-tribunes are to originate all laws respecting revenues, peace, and war; the chamber-of-senators to form the civil, criminal, and ecclesiastical codes; the chamber-of-censors, to watch against any infringement of the constitution, and over the execution of its laws and treaties. The collective duties of the three chambers are to nominate the first president, and to confirm his successor; to invest the president with extraordinary powers in cases of emergency; and to regulate the department of police. The executive consists of a president, latter hold office at the pleasure of the president; but he does not nominate the magistrates, judges, or any ecclesiastical dignitary. The judges are perfectly independent. The provincial administration of the republic is by prefects, governors, corregidors, justices-of-peace, and alcaldes. The state religion is the Roman Catholic; and the republic is divided into the three bishoprics of Chuquisaca, La Paz, and Santa Cruz.—The military force consists of 17,000 men, of whom about 5,000 are regular troops.-The revenue in 1832 amounted to 1,586,026 dollars; and the debt to nearly the same sum. Administrative divisions.] The republic of B. is politically divided into 5 departments; and each of these departments is sub-divided into provinces. Arequipa, History.] The republic of B. was erected into an independent state by a declaration of the citizens, of 5th August, 1825, and named in honour of the great South American liberator, Bolivar. The territory formerly belonged to Peru; but was detached from that country, and annexed to the vice-royalty of Buenos Ayres, in 1778. The mother-country made a desperate effort to preserve her sovereignty of this part of her American possessions; but the revolutionists, aided by a Colombian force under Bolivar, finally triumphed in the contest. On the 11th August, 1825, a constituent congress, assembled at Chuquisaca, decreed to form a new republic, to be named Bolivia, and to call its capital, Sucre, after the general in whom the government was vested for the time being, with the title of captain-general and grand-marshal of Ayacucho. This congress, having solicited Bolivar to prepare a constitution or fundamental code, dissolved itself on the 6th of October. The constitution proposed by Bolivar, and which was adopted by congress, in 1826, vested the executive power in a president chosen for life, with the privilege of naming his successor, and the legislative power, in three bodies, a senate, tribune, and censors. But this constitution was soon abolished. Sucre, at the time of the adoption of the constitution, resigned his discretionary power, and was elected president; but he sent in his resignation to the congress which assembled on 3d August, 1828, and, having returned to Colombia, he was assassinated near Pasto, in June, 1830. On the retirement of Sucre, General Velasco filled the office of president till the meeting of the convention on the 16th December. The convention displaced Velasco, and elected General Blanco, president, and General Looysa, vicepresident; but a revolution soon followed, which resulted in the deposition and death of Blanco, Jan. 1st, 1829. A temporary government was now established, with Velasco at its head; and Santa Cruz was ultimately elected president or protector. In 1838, the constitution was remodelled. A series of revolutions and counter-revolutions have continued to agitate this unfortunate territory almost up to the date at which we pen this. The present president Belzu is understood to have surrendered his own dictatorial power to that of the laws, and to have re-established the constitution of 1838, which had been for some time in abeyance.
BOLIVIA, a town of Gasconade co., U. S., in the state of Missouri. Pop. in 1840, 779.—Also a town of Jefferson co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. in 1840, 485.
BOLKACS, a village of Transylvania, in the district of Küküllö. The wine produced in the environs is reputed the best in the principality.

BOLKENHAIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, cap. of the circle of the same name, in the regency and 20 m. S of Liegnitz, on an affluent of the Katzbach. Pop. in 1837, 1,630. It contains a Catholic and a Lutheran church, an hospital, and the ruins of the ancient castle of Bolkoburg, and pos- BOL sesses manufactories of cloth, linen and ribbon, and breweries. Thread forms its chief article of commerce. The circle possesses 3 towns, 38 villages, and 26,131 inhabitants; and contains extensive mines of copper and silver.
BOLKOF. See Bolchov.
BOLKOWITZ. See Polkwitz.

BOLL, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 6 m. SSW of Göppingen. Pop. 1,492. It has mineral springs and baths which are much frequented. Sulphur and curious petrifactions are found in the environs.
BOLL. See BULLE.
BOLLAND, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 13 m. NW of Verviers. Pop. 606. It contains manufactories of serge and hosiery.
BOLLENA, a town of Savoy, in the prov. and 20 m. NNE of Nice, near the r. bank of the Visubia.
BOLLENE, a canton, commune, and town of and dye-works. The environs abound with antique remains.
BOLLEN FEE, a township in the parish of Wilmslow, in Cheshire, 6 m. SW of Stockport. Area 2,360 acres. Pop. 2,212.

BOLLINGEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 3 m. NE of Berne, at the foot of the Bolligerberg. Pop. 1,500. It possesses a much frequented mineral bath. In the environs are powder and paper mills, tanneries, forges, &c.
BOLLINGSTED, a town of Denmark, in the duchy and 10 m. NW of Sleswick.

BOLLINGTON, a township in the parishes of Bowden and Rostherne, in Cheshire, 5 m. N by W of Nether-Knutsford. Area 400 acres. Pop. 316. —Also a chapelry in the p. of Prestbury, in Cheshire, 3 m. N by E of Macclesfield. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 4,350.
BOLLNÆS, a town of Sweden, in the amt of Gefle, near the confluence of the Waxna and Ljusna, 27 m. W of Soderhamn.
BOLLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia, on a height 35 m. E of Orense.
BOLLOM, a hamlet in Clarebury p., in Nottinghamshire. Pop. 103.
BOLLULLOS-DE-LA-IMITACION, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 22 m. WSW of Seville.

BOLLWILLER, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Upper Rhine, cant. of Soultz, 16 m. S of Colmar. It has manufactories of cotton and spinning-mills; and contains an extensive horticultural garden, deemed one of the finest in France.

BOLLY, a town of Hungary, in the Baranyer comitat, 10 m. W of Mohacz, and 15 m. ESE of Funkirchen. BOLMSŒ, a village of Sweden, in the amt of Jonkoping, on an island of the same name, in lake Bolmen, 40 m. WNW of Wexio. It was formerly the residence of the governors of Smaaland.
BOLNEY, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SW of Cuckfield. Area 3,550 acres. Pop. 713.
BOLNHURST, a parish in Bedfordshire, 63 m. N by E of Bedford. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 344.
BOLNIKI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the dist. and 50 m. N of Vilna, and 12 m. NE of Vilkomir.

BOLNISSI, a district of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Georgia, and district of Somketia. It comprises three villages, named Upper, Middle, and Lower B. The latter is situated on the r. bank of the Poladaouri. The district contains extensive mines of iron and silver; and abounds with acidulous springs. Its mountainous regions afford excellent wine.

BOLO'GNA, a legation, or administrative district of the States-of-the-Church; bounded on the N by Ferrara; on the E by Ravenna; on the S by the Apennines, which divide it from Tuscany; and on the W by the duchy of Modena. Its length from SW to NE is about 50 m.; its breadth about 30 m. It is watered by the Reno which flows towards the Po; and in its NE part, where skirted by the Po, is low and marshy. The superficies is estimated at 3,358,305 sq. tavola censuale of 1,000 sq. metres, or 1,196 sq. yards, each. The pop. in 1832 was estimated at 324,000. It is a fertile district, rich in corn, wine, fruit, flax, silk, and cattle. Bolo'GNA, the cap. of the above legation, and next to Rome the chief city in the Papal states, is situated in N lat. 44° 29′ 54″, E long. 11° 20′ 40″ [Zach and Fallon, 1836]; 25 m. SW of Ferrara; 22½ m. SE of Modena; 49 m. N of Florence; 182 m. NNW of Rome; at an alt. of 399 ft. above sea-level. It is situated in a beautiful plain at the foot of the Apennines, on the rivers Savona and Reno, the former of which washes its walls, while the latter runs in several branches through the city, and communicates with the Po by means of a canal. It is of an oblong form, is surrounded with a lofty brick wall pierced with 12 gates; and is about 5 or 6 miles in circuit. The streets are narrow, and rather gloomy, from the fronts of the houses being built upon arcades. The houses are almost entirely of brick, but tolerably built. The pillars of these arcades or porticos, are irregular in different houses, -some of them being high, others low, some square, and some round,— and some of stone, while others are of wood. The streets where the carriages pass each other are considerably lower than the porticos, like 'the rows' at Chester. The houses are flat roofed, with a parapet towards the streets, and are covered with tiles.—The public buildings of this city are equally remarkable for the beauty of their architecture and for their internal decorations.—The tower of Asinelli, built by Gerardo Asinelli in 1110, stands in the centre of the city. It is 320 ft. high, and is the loftiest in Italy, excepting the cupola of St. Peter's. It inclines from the perpendicular about 3 ft. 2 in. Its original height was 476 ft.; but after an earthquake in 1416 one-fourth was taken down. From the top of this tower, the fertile plain of Lombardy appears like a map beautifully illuminated, with gilded towers and buildings of various character; and the Apennines exhibit a semicircle of pleasing forms, robed in snow in the earlier part of the year. Near this tower is the leaning tower of Garisenda, which is 144 ft. high, and inclines about 6 ft. 6 inches from a vertical line. One of the finest buildings of B. is the Palazzo del Publico, in which the vice-legate, the gonfalomere, and other officers-of-state had their apartments, and in which the courts-of-justice are held. It stands in the great market-place, presenting a front of 218 paces. A brass statue of Gregory executed by Minganti, and weighing 11,300 lbs., is over the entrance; and at the left of the entrance is another statue of Boniface VIII. The museum of Aldrovandi is preserved in this palace.—Many noble families reside at B., where they have fine palaces. The palace of Caprara is particularly interesting to travellers.—The palace of Fava is enriched with the works of the Caraccis. The palaces of Bentivoglio, Bevilacque, Lambertini, Baciocchi, Ercolani, and I BOL Sampieri, have valuable galleries of paintings and works of art.—The churches of B. are both numerous and splendid. They are said to amount to 74, most of which contain some of the finest productions of the Italian masters.—The Cathedral, or Il Duomo, is a modern structure, elegantly finished both within and without. It contains a great number of monuments, and is peculiarly rich in marble decorations. —The church of the Dominicans contains, in a magnificent chapel, the monument of St. Dominico, who died in 1221. It is of white marble, and adorned with beautiful basso relievos by Michael Angelo. The convent of the Dominicans contains a library of 83,000 vols.—The largest church in B. is the church of San Petronio, in which Charles V. was crowned in 1530. It is 360 ft. long, and 154 ft. broad; but, according to its original plan, was to have been 712 ft. in length. The principal curiosity in this church is the meridian line drawn by Dominique Cassini in 1656, and renewed in 1695. It consists of inlaid pieces of red and white marble, about 3 or 4 inches broad; the pieces on which the signs of the zodiac are cut are a foot square. The marbles were renewed in 1776, under the direction of M. Zanotti. The gnomon is 83 ft. high; and a circular image of the sun, about 8 in. diam., is admitted through a round aperture in the roof.—The university of B. is said to have been founded in 433, by the emperor Theodosius II.; others are of opinion that it owed its origin to Charlemagne. The first professor of civil law at B. was Ireneri or Irnerio, who was brought here by Lothario. In the time of Andrea and Azzo,—the one professor of canon, and the other of civil law, —it is said that there were no fewer than 10,000 students at this university. The public college, or university, called Il Studio, is 213 paces long, and was built by Barocci of Vignola. In this establishment, the principal objects deserving of notice are the astronomical school, containing a model of the Copernican system; the meridian line, cut through a wall a foot thick, under the direction of Manfredi; the observatory; the library of 80,000 vols.; the school of experimental philosophy; the academy of sculpture; the academy of painting; the anatomical theatre; and the museum, which contains a large collection of antiquities and natural curiosities. The chairs now filled in this university are those of theology, law, medicine, philosophy, mathematics, and the belles lettres. The faculty of medicine is in high repute. B. also possesses an academy of the fine arts, a scientific institute, an architectural college, an extensive musical library, and a philharmonic lyceum. B. has given birth to a number of eminent men, who have distinguished themselves in the various departments of literature and science. Azzo, Odofredus, Campeggi, Paleotti, and Ireneri, were among its distinguished lawyers; and Mondini Achillini and Marcello Malpighi, among its anatomists and physicians. Natural history is under obligations to its citizens Aldrovandi, and Ferdinand Marsigli; and Peter Crescenzi was the first of the modern Italians who wrote a considerable work on the art of agriculture. Ghedini, Manfredi, Zanotti, Beccari, Canterzani, Monti, and Galvani, hold a distinguished place in mathematics and natural philosophy. No fewer than 8 popes have been natives of B. A very considerable trade in damasks, satins, taffeties, silks, ribbons, and velvets, has been carried on by B. since 1341. The silk-mills are driven by the river Reno, which also gives motion to a great quantity of machinery for various other purposes. The white and coloured crapes and gauzes of B. are in high estimation; the work of its gold-beaters is much admired; and its manufactures of paper and crapes, gauzes, and cards of B. were exported to France and Germany; and its hemp and flax went to Venice for the manufacture of sails and cordage. Artificial flowers of all kinds are made in great abundance by the nuns in B.; and a large trade is carried on from this city in hams, dried tongues, sausages, maccaroni, olives, perfumes, wash-balls, wax-candles, liqueurs, essences, and leather and glass bottles. About 50,000 lbs. of sulphuric acid, and 10,000 lbs. of nitric acid, are annually made in B. The surrounding country is rich and fertile, and the neighbourhood resembles an immense garden. The vineyards are divided by rows of elms and mulberry-trees. The melons, olives, and tobacco, are particularly celebrated; and hemp grows here to the height of 12 and 13 ft., and has been mistaken by travellers for plantations of young ash trees. The trade of B. has been much facilitated by the canal, called Naviglio, which joins the branch of the Reno that runs through the city, with the lake of Valle di Marara, from which the merchandise of B. is sent to Ferrara, and other places situated on the Po. B. is deemed one of the coldest places in Italy in winter; and one of the hottest in summer. The lingua Bolognese, spoken not only by the common people, but also frequently by people of the middle ranks, differs considerably from the purest Italian. Good society in B. is made up of whatever is most distinguished among the nobility, professors, bankers, and merchants; even the casino is open to the cittadini as well as to the nobles. History.] B. was the Felsina of the Etruscans. The Romans changed its name into Bononia. In the time of the Roman republic, it was but a small town; it is only mentioned in Greek and Latin authors as deriving its name from the Gaulish tribe of the Boii. It would appear from Martial, that, under the first emperors, the Bolognese were particularly polished; we are expressly informed, moreover, by that author, that one of the most lucrative trades in this place was that of a shoemaker. Though inferior to the surrounding cities, B. gradually rose in importance. It acquired celebrity from the interview between Octavius, Anthony, and Lepidus, in an island formed by the Reno, and likewise from several events during the civil war of these triumvirs. It was not, however, till the 12th and 13th cent. that B. became a great and a learned city. It enjoyed a species of independence under the emperors of Germany till 1278, when it was given over to Pope Nicholas III., with the reservation of some special privileges to its citizens. Internal discord, and wars with neighbouring states, kept B. in an unsettled condition, till Julian II., taking advantage of the Venetian war, made himself absolute master of it, expelled the family of the Bentivoglio, and annexed it to the Papal dominions. It was stipulated, however, that the Bolognese should have a nuncio at the court of Rome, and an auditor in the Rota; that the town should not be overawed by a citadel; and that the effects of the citizens should not be liable to confiscation. Its ecclesiastical affairs were to be decided by the archbishop, while its civil affairs were under the direction of a cardinal-legate from Rome, with a vice-legate, and other assistants. The police and revenue of the town were managed by a council of 50 senators, whose president was called the gonfaloniere, from his carrying the gonfalone, or standard of the republic. Each senator took this office by turns, and continued in it for two months: his authority, however, was merely nominal, as the cardinal-legate influenced every decision. In 1796, after this city was taken by the French, Bologna, Ferrara, Modena, and Reggio, entered into a treaty to form a republic under the name of Respublica Cispadona. Some time afterwards, however, these cities united with Lombardy to form the Cisalpine, afterwards called the Italian republic, which was finally transformed by the will of its founder into the kingdom of Italy, in which B. was the cap. of the dep. Del Reno. In 1831, a revolt broke out at B. against the Papal authority, which was put down by Austrian interference.—Italy in its original Glory, Ruin, and Revival. By E. Wircupp. Lond., 1660.—Keysler's Travels, vol. iii.—Drummond's Travels, letter ii.—Denina's Tableau Historique Statistique et Moral de la Haute Italie, sect. xvi.—Reichard's Guide des Voyageur en Europe.—Stolberg's Travels, vol. i.—Moore's View of Society, &c., in Italy, vol. i.— Valery, Voyage Literaire.—Rampoldi, Corografia dell' Italia.—Cadell's Journey.
BOLOLE, a town on the E coast of the island of Celebes, in the Eastern seas, in S lat. 3° 22'.
BOLOLO, a town near the SE corner of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 13° 7', E long. 17°.
BOLOR. See BELUR-TAGH.

BOLOTHANA, a town of Sardinia, situated in BOL playing-cards are reckoned very superior. The i the angle formed by the Margine mountains and those of Goceano, 16 m. W of Bosa. It has a pop. of 3,200. There are numerous noraghes or cairns in the vicinity of this place.
BOLSAS. See Balsas.
BOLSCHAIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, situated on the river Irtisch, 240 m. ESE of Tobolsk.

BOLSCHAIA REKA, a river of Kamtschatka, which rises in a lake about 120 m. from its mouth. It is at first known by the name of Natshelne, and in its course is augmented by a number of streams, the most considerable of which is the Bystrea, or Rapid river, flowing into it about 20 m. before its efflux into the sea of Okhotzk, in N lat. 54° 52'. The Bolschaia is the only river flowing into this sea that can be navigated from the mouth nearly to its source; which, however, is not accomplished without difficulty, on account of its rapidity, and the number of islands in its channel. At high water vessels of considerable size may enter, the tide then rising nearly 10 ft.; but it is unsheltered, and, from the lowness of the surrounding land, is exposed to every wind. The N coast is frequently overflowed, and the channel itself sometimes alters, both in depth and direction. There is a lighthouse S of the entrance of the river.
BOLSCHAKINA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, 68 m. S of Orlenga.

BOLSE'NA, a small town of the States-of-the- Church, in the deleg. of Viterbo, situated on a lake of the same name, the ancient Lacus Volsiniensis, 10 m. WSW of Orvieto, 54 m. NW by N of Rome. Pop. 2,000. It is but a poor place; but is encompassed by a pretty high wall, flanked with towers, and by a broad ditch. The bishopric of B. is now removed to Orvieto. The lake forms an oval of about 21 m. in circuit, and abounds in fish, particularly large eels, and aquatic fowls. It is surrounded by mountains, which, rising in a gradual ascent, and being covered with lofty oaks, present the appearance of a spacious amphitheatre. In the lake are two islands called Bisentina and Martana. It discharges itself by the Marta, the ancient Lartes, into the sea near Corneto. The surrounding territory is unhealthy, malaria prevailing on the borders of the lake. - The Volsinii of the Etruscans, which was razed by Flaccus, B. C. 266, stood near the present site of B.
BOLSHOI, an island on the coast of Siberia, to the W of the mouth of the Lena, in N lat. 73°, E long. 124° 40′.

BOLSON-DE-MAPIMI, a mountainous tract of Mexico, forming the W part of the prov. of Cohahuila, and stretching along the E side of the Rio Conchos, and of the upper part of the Rio Brave del Norte, above the junction of the Conchos. It comprises upwards of 3,000 sq. leagues, and is chiefly in the possession of wandering tribes of Apaches. A low country intervenes between the B. de M. and the gulf of Mexico.
BOLSOE, an island of Norway, in the Fannefiord, one of the inlets of Romsdals-fiord.

BOLSOVER, a parish and town in Derbyshire, 5½ m. E of Chesterfield. Area of p. 6,060 acres. Pop. 1,512. The town occupies the summit of a steep hill, from which the view is varied and extensive. The church is an ancient structure, containing a fine sepulchral chapel belonging to the family of Cavendish. B. was celebrated at one period for the making of steel-buckles; but this branch of trade has long since declined. The manufacture of tobaccopipes is carried on to some extent here, a kind of clay suitable for that purpose being found in the neighbourhood; the fire-bricks also of this place are held in high repute. A fine building-stone is found in this neighbourhood. The professional gentlemen employed in selecting a proper material to be employed in the new houses of parliament, reported, that "for durability; for crystalline character, combined with a close approach to the equivalent proportions of carbonate of lime and carbonate of magnesia; for uniformity of structure; facility and economy in conversion; and for advantage of colour, the magnesian limestone, or dolomite of B. moor and its neighbourhood is, in their opinion, the most fit and proper material."

BOLSWAARD, or Bolswert, a very old town of Holland, in West Friesland, situated on a canal 5 m. E of the Vlie-stroom, or channel of the Zuyder- Zee, 10 m. N of Sloten, 7 m. SSE of Harlingen. Pop. 3,374. It was formerly of the Hanse confederation, and has a port about 3 m. distant. B. was, in former times, a place of considerable extent, and being built in a scattered form, is still fully 2 m. in circuit. It carries on a thriving trade in butter, and in fine woollen stuffs.—B. is said to have been founded in the year 713 by Bolswine, son of Radbode, king of Friesland, from whom it took its name. It was several times laid waste by the Danes and Normans, and nearly burnt to the ground in 1475 and 1515. Near the town there was formerly a Cistercian abbey, called Sude-clooster, where the Munster Anabaptists took refuge in 1534. In this abbey was interred William IV., count of Holland, slain by the Frieslanders in 1345.
BOLTBY, a parish and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NE of Thirsk. Area 3,140 acres. Pop. 320.
BOLT-HEAD, a signal station on the coast of Devonshire, in N lat. 50° 13′ 15″, W long. 3° 48′.
BOLT-HEAD, a cape of Australia, on the NE coast, to the SSW of Cape Grenville, in S lat. 12° 15', E long. 143°.
BOLTIGEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 22 m. SSW of Berne, near the l. bank of the Simmen.

BOLTINO, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Macedonia, in the sanjak and 71 m. SSE of Monastir, and 27 m. SSE of Bogotzico, near the r. bank of the Aias. BOLTON, BOLTON-LE-MOORS, OF GREAT BOLTON, a large parish, and a recently enfranchised borough, in the hund. of Salford, co.-palatine of Lancaster; by coach-road 197 m. NW of London; 11 m. NW of Manchester; and 44 m. SSW of Lancaster; in N lat. 53° 33'. Parish.] The parish forms the NW part of the hundred; and comprises the chapelries of Blackrod, Little B., Bradshaw, Darcy-Lever, Rivington, and Turton; and the townships of Anglezarke, Great B., Breightmet, Edgeworth, Entwistle, Harwood, Little Lever, Longworth, Quarlton, Sharples, and Tonge with Haulgh, and the hamlet of Lostock. Area of p. 31,390 acres. Pop. in 1801, 29,826; in 1811, 39,701; in 1821, 50,197; in 1831, 60,034; in 1841, 73,905. Its name is derived from its original situation in the middle of dreary moors, which are now, however, to a great extent under cultivation. Three small streams, the Croal, the Bradshaw, and the Tonge, unite at B., and, in one stream, join the Irwell, 13 m. SSE of the town. Among the hills in this parish are Rivington-pike, a well-known landmark, Winter-hill, Horrocks-moor, and Edgeworthmoor. Coal and freestone are abundant in this district, in which upwards of 40 pits are wrought; veins of lead ore and calamine have been discovered at Rivington, but have not hitherto proved productive. -The B. poor-law union, in 1831, embraced 26 parishes and townships, with a total area of 43,640 acres; and a population of 83,349 souls, inhabiting 15,029 houses, or, on an average, 5.8 to each house. Kenyon, crossing in its progress the Wigan and Leigh ~ BOLTON. By the census of 1841, the population of the district embraced in the union was returned at 97,529 souls; and the number of houses at 20,392. The money expended for the maintenance of the poor of this union in the year ending March 25th, 1838, was £8,889; in that ending March 25th, 1846, £24,987. Borough.] The borough of B. consists of the townships of Great and Little B., which are separated by the Croal, and the township of Haulgh. Pop. in 1841, 50,163. The borough as thus described, with the exception of part of Little B. to the N of the town, returns two members to parliament under the Reform bill. In 1836 the number of electors was 1,340; in 1837, 1,405; in 1842-3, 1,207. The town has extended greatly of late years. It received a charter in 1838; and is now governed by a mayor, 36 common councillors, and 12 aldermen. A commission of the peace was granted to the borough in February 1839. In consequence of serious riots which took place here in 1839, a special police bill for the borough of B., and part of Little B., was carried through parliament. The parochial affairs of the two townships of Great and Little B. are managed by separate bodies. The appearance of the town is by no means imposing. It possesses one large and good street; and in the environs several rows of neat houses have sprung up of late years. There are several good libraries and news-rooms, and a theatre and assembly-rooms. The exchange buildings is a neat stone edifice, erected in 1825, in which year a mechanics' institution was also founded.—Pop. of the township of Great B., in 1801, 12,549; in 1831, 28,299; in 1841, 33,610. Area 820 acres. A. P., in 1815, £27,887; in 1829, £63,865; in 1836, at rack rent, £80,000; in 1842-3, £109,665.—Pop. of the chapelry of Little B., in 1801, 4,867; in 1831, 12,896; in 1841, 16,153. Area 1,450 acres. A. P., in 1815, £11,747; in 1829, £23,680; in 1836, at rack rent, £41,840; in 1842-3, £43,422.—Pop. of Haulgh, in 1831, 1,300; in 1841, 1,266. Area 268 acres. Manufactures.] B. owes its prosperity to its manufactures. The most important of these is the cottonmanufacture, a branch of trade to which the invention of the mule-jenny by Samuel Crompton—who long resided in the immediate neighbourhood of Bolton at a house called 'Hall-in-the-Wood'-gave an astonishing impulse. This machine combines the powers of the spinning-jenny and the water-frame. The ingenious mechanic was rewarded by two donations of £105, and £400, raised by private subscription, and subsequently, in 1812, by a parliamentary grant of £5,000. The spinning factories here were long on a small scale, in consequence of the scarcity of water; but, since the introduction of the steamengine as a motive power, and the adoption of improved machinery, they have become very extensive. In 1838 there were 69 cotton-mills, employing 9,918 hands, and 1 flax-mill in which 261 persons were engaged, within this parish. In January 1842, the number of mills was returned at 50; but of these 30 were idle or working half-time. Here are also very large bleaching-grounds, some paper-mills and chemical works, and numerous iron foundries and machine-factories. In 1836 the number of hands employed in B. as iron-founders, engineers, mill-wrights, and machine-makers, was 2,230. There are above 20 large bleach-fields within 5 m. of B., at which upwards of 6,000,000 pieces of cloth are annually bleached. The market-days are Saturday and Monday; fairs for horned cattle, horses, and cheese, are held on July 30th and 31st, and October 13th and 14th. The market-place is a spacious square area in Great B.—The B. and Leigh railroad joins this borough to the Liverpool and Manchester line, at canal.—The Manchester, B., and Bury railway, which commences in New Bailey-street, Salford, and runs almost parallel with the Bury and B. canal, through the vale of the Irwell, establishes a direct line of communication with Manchester 123 m. in length.—The B. and Preston railway runs from the B. terminus of the B. and Manchester line, in Trinity-street, to Euxton, 31 m. beyond Chorley, where it joins the North Union railway. Its length is 141 m.—The Manchester, B., and Bury canal, unites these three towns by a water-communication 15 m. in length. History.] The importance of B. is dated from 1337, when a number of emigrant Flemings settled here, and introduced various manufactures, especially that of woollen cloth. In the reign of Henry VIII. Leland gives the following description of Bolton:- "Bolton-upon-Moore market standeth most by cottons and coarse yarne. Divers villages in the moores about Bolton do make cottons." These 'cottons,' it is well-known, were a kind of woollen fabric. Blome, who wrote about a century after, says, "Bolton hath a market on Mondays, which is very good for clothing and provisions, and it is a place of great trade for fustians." By the munificence of Henry the Stanley family became possessed of the principal part of the town of B.; but a considerable portion was confiscated during the Commonwealth. At the commencement of the civil war, the inhabitants declared themselves in favour of the Parliament, in whose interest the town was garrisoned. In 1644, Prince Rupert and the earl of Derby invested B., and made an attempt to take it by storm, but were repulsed; the earl, however, returned with a reinforcement, and got possession of the place. It was subsequently given up; and after the battle of Worcester, the earl of Derby was beheaded here, on the 15th of October, 1651, in pursuance of the sentence of a military tribunal at Chester.

BOLTON, a parish in Cumberland, 6 m. SSW of Wigton. Area 8,760 acres. Pop. 1,211.—Also a township in Edlingham p., in Northumberland. Pop. 128.—Also a chapelry in Morland p., in Westmoreland. Pop. 383.—Also a township in Bishop-Wilton p., in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. NW of Pocklington. Pop. 98.—Also a township in Calverley p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Pop. 683.
BOLTON, a small parish in Haddingtonshire, on the Gifford or Coalstone water, a branch of the Tyne. Pop. in 1801, 252; in 1841, 341.
BOLTON, a township of Lower Canada, in Stanstead co., on the W side of Lake Memphremagog. Pop. 1,008 in 1827. It has a mountainous surface.

BOLTON, a township of Chittenden co., U. S., in the state of Vermont, 19 m. NNW of Montpelier. It lies on the W range of the Green mountains, and, with the exception of the district watered by the Onion, is generally incapable of cultivation. Pop. in 1840, 470.—Also a township in Worcester co., Massachusetts, 33 m. W of Boston. It is situated between the Concord and Nashua, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,186.—Also a township of Tolland co., Connecticut. It lies on an elevated range of hills, drained by branches of the Hop. The soil is gravelly loam, and affords excellent pasturage. Pop. in 1840, 739.—Also a township of Warren co., New York, 73 m. N of Albany. It presents a mountainous surface, extending to Lake George on the E, and is watered by the Schroon, a tributary of the Hudson. In the interior are several large ponds. The soil is fertile, but is to a great extent densely covered with wood. Pop. in 1840, 937.

BOLTON-ABBEY, a township in Skipton p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5½ m. NE of Skipton. Pop. 127. The vicinity is a beautiful and romantic country intersected by the Wharfe, and adorned with the venerable ruins of B. priory.
BOLTON-BY-BOWLAND, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NE of Clitheroe, on the N bank of the Ribble. Area 4,940 acros. Pop. 933.
BOLTON-CASTLE, a chapelry in Wensley p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NW by W of Middleham. Area 5,160 acres. Pop. 230.
BOLTON-UPON-DEARNE, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. N by E of Rotterham. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 671. BOM

BOLTON-PERCY, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by S of Tadcaster. Area 7,320 acres. Pop. 1,040, of whom 241 were in the township of B. which is intersected by the York and North Midland railway, by which it is 7 m. 40 chains from York.
BOLTON-LE-SANDS, a parish in Lancashire, 4 m. N of Lancaster. Area 7,630 acres. Pop. 1,774, of whom 671 were in the township of B.
BOLTON-UPON-SWALE, a chapelry in the parish of Catterick, N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. E by S of Richmond. Area 500 acres. Pop. 96.

BOLVA, a river of Russia in Europe, which takes its rise in the gov. of Kaluga, in the dist. of Masalsk, crosses that of Jizdra, and, entering the gov. of Orel, unites with the Desna, 3 m. NW of Briansk, after a course from N to S of about 105 m. It is navigable during a considerable portion of the year, and forms the chief means of the transit of the produce of the iron mines of Loudinskae and Pessotchinskae.
BOLY. See Boli.
BOLYA, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Transylvania, and circle of Ober-Abba.
BOLZANO. See Botzen.
BOM-ABRIGO, an island of Brazil, off the coast of the prov. of San Paulo, to the S of the entrance of the bay of Tarapande, in S lat. 15° 6' 49".
BOMAL, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, on the r. bank of the Ourte, 30 m. NNW of Bastogne.
BOMARZO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 10 m. NE of Viterbo. Pop. 1,200.

BOMBA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Citra, in the dist. and 21 m. WSW of Vasto, on Mount Pallano, on the r. bank of the Sangro. Pop. 2,357. It contains a handsome parish church, one of the finest in the prov. The environs afford oil and excellent wine; and contain numerous colossal remains of antiquity.

BOMBA, or Bombah, a small island, or cluster of islands, of the Mediterranean, in the large gulf of the same name, on the coast of Tripoli, in N lat. 32° 22' 28", and E long. 23°. These islands form a safe and capacious harbour known as Port Bomba.
BOMBAÇA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, an affluent of the Rio Doce, which it joins on the l. bank, after a course of 36 m.

BOMBARACK (CAPE), or MUBARIC, a headland on the coast of the Persian gulf, in N lat. 26° 20', bearing N by W 3 W 14 or 15 leagues from Cape Jask. About 3 m. inland from the beach here is the Kope Mubaric, or 'Fortunate hill,' an insulated rock which rises to a height of 700 or 800 ft.
BOMBAS POINT, a promontory of Brazil, on the coast of the prov. of Catherina, in S lat. 27° 5', and W long. 48° 37'.

BOMBAY, the principal British settlement on the western coast of India, and the cap. of the presidency of Bombay, situated upon a small island formerly comprehended in the province of Aurungabad, in 18° 56' N lat., and 72° 57' E long., 1,300 m. travelling distance, and 1,050 m. direct distance SW from Calcutta; 770 m. travelling distance, and 650 m. direct distance NW from Madras; and 1,640 m. E by N from Aden. It derives its name and all its importance from its excellent harbour, which is enclosed by a string of islands arranged in a crescent form. Island. The island of B. itself is about 8 m. long from N to S, and from 2 to 3 m. wide, and does not exceed 20 m. in circumf. Area including Colaba and Old Woman's island, about 18.5 sq. m. Its frame-work, so to speak, consists of two ridges of whinstone rock, running parallel to each other, and united at the N and S extremities by low cross belts of sandstone. The sea, and the Goper river, it is said, once flowed between these ridges; and at a later period, after the retreating ocean had left the whole an island, a forest of cocoa trees covered the intervening valley. The lower parts of the interior of the island are still from 10 to 12 ft. under the level of the sea; and spring-tides are only prevented submerging a great part of the island by artificial embankments and flood-gates; while much of the surface during the rainy season is converted into a marsh. The island of B., "as well as most of those in its neighbourhood," says Bishop Heber, "is apparently little more than a cluster of detached rocks, which have been joined together by the gradual progress of coral reefs, aided by sand thrown up by the sea, and covered with the vegetable mould occasioned by the falling leaves of the sea-loving cocoa. The interior consists of a long but narrow tract of low ground, which has evidently been, in the first instance, a salt lagoon, gradually filled up by the progress which I have mentioned, and from which the high tides are still excluded, only by artificial embankments. This tract is a perfect marsh during the rainy season, and in a state of high rice cultivation. The higher ground is mere rock and sand, but covered with cocoa and toddy-palms where they can grow. There is scarcely any open or grass-land in the island, except the esplanade before the fort, and the exercising ground at Matoonga." Mrs. Roberts has given a more minute and more flattering account of the general scenery of the island. "The variety of the foliage of the cocoa-nut, the crab, and others, the manner of their growth, differing according to the different directions taken, and the exquisite grouping which continually occurs, prevents," that lady remarks, in her lively papers on India, "the monotony which their profusion might otherwise create, the general effect being, under all circumstances, absolutely perfect. Though the principal, the palm is far from being the only tree, and, while frequently forming whole groves, it is as frequently blended with two species of cypress, the peepul, mango, banian, wild cinnamon, and several others. In addition to the splendour of its wood and water, Bombay is embellished by fragments of dark rock, which force themselves through the soil, roughening the sides of the hills, and giving beauty to the precipitous heights, and shelving beach. Though the island is comparatively small, extensively cultivated, and thickly inhabited, it possesses its wild and solitary places, its ruins deeply seated in thick forests, and its lonely hills covered with rock, and thinly wooded by the eternal palm-tree; hills which, in consequence of the broken nature of the ground, and their cavernous recesses, are difficult of access. It is in these fastnesses that the hyenas find secure retreats, and the Parsees construct their 'towers of silence.' There is little, or indeed nothing, in the scenery that comes under the denomination of jungle, the island being intersected in every part with excellent roads, macadamized with the stone that abounds so conveniently for the purpose. These roads are sometimes skirted by walls of dark stone, which harmonise well with the trees that never fail to spread their shade above; at others, with beautiful hedge rows, while across the flats and along the esplanade, a water-course or a paling form the enclosures." On the N the island of B. is connected with the larger island of Salsette (a) by a causeway originally constructed in 1805, to enable the natives of the latter island to transport their agricultural produce to the markets of B. On a narrow neck of land, near the SE extremity, stands the town (b) and fort (c). At the extremity of this isthmus, and connected with it by a ridge of rocks which rise near to the surface of the water at ordinary tides, and on which a causeway, overflowed at spring-tides, has been formed, is Colaba or Old Woman's island, (d), bearing on its S end a lighthouse, with a lanthorn 130 ft. above water, bearing S 36° W, and distant 2½ m. from B. fort. This lighthouse is in N lat. 18° 53' 45". This island is also separated into two parts which are only connected by a causeway; the smaller part to the N is that which is properly called Old Woman's island. The opposite side of the entrance to the harbour is formed by Caranja island, the low and wooded surface of which is diversified by two remarkable hills. The channel between Colaba and Caranja is about 3 m. wide, and from 7 to 8 fath. deep. On theW side of the isthmus and of Colaba is a large bay (e) called Black bay; the W horn of which terminates in a hill called Malabar point (f), forming also the SW extremity of the island. Butcher's island (h) is nearly opposite to B. castle, at the distance of 3 m.; and about 2 m. from this is the celebrated island of Elephanta. a h 0 0 MILES City.] The town or city of B. is built within the fortifications, and is nearly 1 m. in length, with a breadth in some places of 1/4 m. Seen from the bunder, or landing-place, where passengers disembark on their arrival here, there is nothing to indicate its wealth or importance. The Scottish church, a white-washed building of no pretensions, is the most striking object from the sea; and the tower of the cathedral rising in the centre, is the only architectural feature in the scene. The houses, within the walls, are, externally, less beautiful than those of Calcutta, having no pillared verandahs, and being disfigured by huge and high pitched conical roofs of red tiles. They are, generally speaking, however, larger, and on the whole better adapted to the climate. Howison says: "I entered B. with the impression that it was the seat of wealth, splendour, fashion, and extravagance; but a stroll upon its esplanade removed the delusion. I believe there are few English watering-places of the third class, that could not produce a better evening turn-out than this Scotch factory. Everything had an air of dinginess, age, and economy, that seemed miserably out of place beneath the ardent clime and radiant skies of Asia. In traversing the town of B., one sees nothing indicative of Eastern magnificence. Its streets are narrow and unpaved; and the buildings are more like merchants' warerooms than dwelling-houses. The European parts of Calcutta consist of a succession of palaces, and Madras abounds with elegant structures; but the best streets in B., I should suppose, scarcely equal the suburbs of its sister-presidencies." The Government-house — which is only employed for the transaction of business-is a large heavy-looking building, occupying one side of an open space in the centre of the town, called the Green. The best houses within the walls, and a respectable church, are situated in this part of the town; to the right of it extends a long and crowded bazaar, in which many of the rich natives have their habitations, in large mansions built after the Asiatic manner. The buildings within the fortifications being too much crowded together for comfort, those belonging to European residents are, for the most part, merely retained as offices and shops, while the families have their houses in the country. Unfortunately the native town of B., or Black town, or Burrah bazaar, as it is called,—"a curious, busy, bustling, dirty quarter, swarming with men and the inferior animals,"-lies between the country or the residences of families in the environs, including Parell (g) the seat of the governor, and the city proper and government offices, and must be passed through before the beautiful scenery of the country on the one hand, or the great centre of business on the other, can be reached. For those, however, who are compelled to live in the immediate vicinity of the government house, the esplanade which stretches between the walls of the fort and the sea, affords a place of retreat in the hot and dry weather. Here it is common to pitch bungalows, tents, and pavilions, forming a gay and fanciful encampment, which at the commencement of the rains is broken up, and gives place with magical celerity to sullen pools of water.—The fortifications of B. are extremely strong towards the sea. The castle is a regular quadrangle. Near the castle—now used as an arsenal—are the docks, which are considered the best in India, and are furnished with slips and basins calculated for vessels of any size. Bombay-built ships have generally proved good durable vessels; some built of teak-wood have been known to last above 50 years; and from the forests of Malabar and Gujerat, B. remen also of B. are considered to be the best in India. The harbour of B. is about 8 m. in diam., and affords safe anchorage for ships of any burden; while the rise of the spring tides—here 14 ft., and sometimes 17 ft.—admits the construction of large wet docks which cannot be easily created on almost any other queen's ships pay a rent when undergoing repairs. Climate.] B. is not a healthy place: it is damp and relaxing; and liver complaints, it is said, are more frequent and fatal here than in any other part of India. When first ceded to the East India company, in 1668, its lagunes and morasses had rendered it an unwholesome swamp covered with jungle; but the superabundant trees have been cut down, and the marshes filled up; and the sea-breeze which sets in every day tempers the solar heat. The mean maximum at 4 P. M. throughout the year is 82°.4; the mean minimum 81°.5, showing a more equable temp. than that of Bengal. The rains last about four months, continuing, with little interruption, from about the end of May to the beginning of October. Heavy mists rise up at sunset from the lower grounds; and the stranger is often kept for whole nights awake by the croaking of the bull-frogs beneath his window. Reptiles of almost every denomination swarm in B., and the insect-tribe is very numerous. Commerce.] B. commands an extensive commerce with the W coast of India, and with the countries situated in the Persian and Arabian gulfs. Its coasting-trade, from Cape Camorin to the gulf of Cutch, employs about 800 vessels of from 10 to 200 tons burden. By this trade, the manufactures of Europe, and the products of China and Bengal, are exported; and cotton, timber, hemp, rice, cocoa-nuts, oil, and grain, are imported, for re-exportation to China, America, and Europe.—To the Arabian and Persian gulfs, B. exports sugar, British goods, cotton, woollen, and spices; and receives in return bullion, raw silk, coffee, gums, dried fruits, horses and pearls. -With China, the trade of B. is very extensive. The staple articles of export are raw cotton and opium; the other articles are betel-nut, sandal-wood, rattans, ivory, pearls, drugs, gums, shark-fins, fish-maws, and edible birds' nests. Of cotton the imports into Canton from B. in 1843 amounted to 231,540 bales; in 1844 it was 229,123 bales; while Madras supplied 66,564 bales, Bengal 46,126 bales, and America 6,192 bales. In 1846, of 258,632 bales imported into Canton, B. supplied 200,255 bales. To the Eastern islands, B. exports opium, indigo, piecegoods, rice, salt, ghee, and hardware, receiving in return various articles chiefly for the Chinese market, and a considerable quantity of bullion.—The principal exports to Britain are raw cotton, and raw silk, wool, piece-goods, coffee, pepper, and spices; while the imports from Britain are cotton-stuffs and yarns, woollen stuffs, hardware, glass, lead, wine, and stationery. The whole value of the exports from B. in 1811-12 was 1,78,08,100 rupees, or £2,003,411; of the imports 2,09,47,585 rupees, or £2,356,603. In B. and Punah there are no fewer than 10 newspapers and magazines published by Hindus. General topography.] It only remains to add a few notices of the general topography of the island. There are three government-residences in B. Besides that within the fort, already noticed, there is another at Malabar point, about 8 m. from the town; but the third and principal government-residence is Parel, about 6 m. from B. The house of Parel is an irregular structure of no architectural pretensions, but the interior is handsome and convenient; and the gardens are exquisitely beautiful. About 31 m. NNW from this is the little town of Mahim (o), chiefly inhabited by Portuguese Christians. The shore of Back bay is the general burial-place of all classes. The Parsis, who practise their ancient and peculiar mode of sepulture, by exposing the body to vultures, have their public sarcophagi, five in number, about 3 m. NW from the fort. The Portuguese village or town of Mazagong (p) is finely situated on the shore, between two hills, and is celebrated for its mango-fruit. History.] The island of B. is supposed to have been the Calliana of the ancients. It was ceded by the Moguls to the Portuguese in 1530, who, on account of its magnificent harbour, bestowed on it the title of Bom Bahia, of which its present name is a corruption. In 1661 it was ceded by Portugal to Great Britain, as a portion of the Infanta's dowry on her marriage with Charles II. In 1668 the Crown transferred this island, which had only proved an expensive possession, to the East India company, who in 1686 transferred the seat of government from Surat to B. In 1687 the administration at B. was declared to be a regency, with unlimited power over all the Company's settlements in the East.
