BOMBAY (Presidency of), one of the presidencies into which the British possessions in India are administratively divided, and which derives its name from the city and island above described. It is the smallest of the three presidencies of British India, and lies between the 14th and 24th parallels of N lat., and the 71st and 77th meridians of E long. On the NW and N it has Cutch and the Rajputana territories; on the NE those of Sinde; on the E the Nizam territories; and on the S the Mysore and Sattara territories, and the presidency of Madras. It comprises the following districts, of the area of which we have given two differing admeasurements: Area, sq. m. Pop. in 1826. Collectorates. sq. m. I. NORTH OF BOMBAY. 1. Ahmedabad. . The above territory has grown out of the original acquisition of Surat and the island of B. In 1773 the adjacent island of Salsette was wrested from the Mahrattas. The district of Baroach was ceded by Dowlat Row Sindiah in December 1803. In 1817, Ahmednuggur, Punah, and Ahmedabad, were formally ceded to the Company, and annexed to this presidency; and in 1818, the Concan, Darwar, and Candeish were added to it. General features.] The districts N of Bombay, all forming part of the ancient subah of Guzerat, embrace an extensive portion of low, wild, sea-coast,— flat, barren, and swampy, and full of creeks and inlets, the haunts of pirates, until repressed by the vigour of the Bombay marine. In the central parts, and towards the E boundary, this region is hilly, and even mountainous, in some places covered with dense forests, in others with low jungle, both affording refuge to marauding tribes and fugitives from all quarters. Here we find the Grasias, the Kulis, the Bhats, the Charans, Angrias, Dheras, Kunbis, Banyans, Jains, Parsis, and Bohras. The Nerbudda, the Tapti, the Sabermutti, the Mahendry, and the Banas, are the principal rivers which intersect the B. presidency. In other districts, and indeed throughout a large portion of Guzerat, we have a flat, fertile, and well-cultivated country, inhabited by a thriving peasantry. Over the district of Cutch, the B. administration exercises a strong political control. It is valuable for its mines of coal and iron, and extent of coast.—The Deccan, which comprises Candeish, Concan, Punah, Ahmednuggur, and Darwar, is said closely to resemble, in its physical features, Hungary in Europe. It is divided by the natives into the Mahals or hilly tracts, which lie along the face of the Ghauts, and the Desh or flat country. It is a picturesque district, intersected by chains of low hills, between which lie narrow and romantic valleys; and here and there opening into extensive plains. The Concan is the low land at the foot of the Ghauts. Candeish presents the greatest extent of a level well-watered surface, but is in many places still covered with jungle. Punah and Ahmednuggur are in great part a tableland, elevated 2,000 ft. above sea-level.—Darwar also is an elevated table-land, and Sattara and the Southern Jaghires are hilly districts, intersected by the Western Ghauts.—The inland mountain-ranges are generally of porphyritic trap, presenting naked mural mation stretches from Delhi to the head of the gulf | prices for years from various parts of India; but he escarpments in many places. A red sandstone forof Cambay; and the whole coast from Goa to the head of that gulf is of basaltic structure. Climate and productions.] The N districts of this presidency are among the most unhealthy in India. Extreme heat, and the exhalations from the marshy jungles, are here very trying to European constitutions, and fever and ague are prevalent. To the S of B. the climate is more congenial to natives of Europe. Yet the mean mortality of the troops within the presidencies is 4.68 per cent., but the loss at Bombay exceeds this, being 5.07 per cent. [Sykes's Tables.]—The animal kingdom in this quarter of India presents us with tigers, panthers, leopards, hyenas, jackals, wild boars, and buffaloes, and the noble elephant still roams in freedom in the forests of the Ghauts. Rice and cotton are the chief articles of culture. Rice is largely grown in the central parts of the presidency. The B. government is peculiarly interested in the cultivation of cotton, as by far the larger proportion of this Indian export is from Bombay: yet it is said the cultivation of cotton throughout the presidency is declining. In 1843, a mercantile house in Bombay wrote as follows: "The crop of Surat and Baroach cotton this year will not be large, and 8,000 acres in the Baroach district alone are believed to be taken out of cotton cultivation, the prices now ruling not remunerating the cultivators." In 1846, three years later, the threatened annihilation of the staple trade of B. caused the governor in council to assemble a committee composed of merchants and Company's servants, before whom it was established, that upon every candy of Surat cotton, costing 80 rupees, or £8, at the port of shipment, the company have 48 rupees, or £4 16s. as land-tax and sea-duty, leaving to the grower in Guzerat 32 rupees, or £3 4s., less than one penny per pound (<math>\frac{3}{4}</math> 45-49d.) as the whole return for his subsistence, his labour, and profit of cultivation; and that in other cotton provinces, where the land-tax is not so excessive as in this, the roads are so execrable, and the transport to the sea is rendered so dilatory and costly that the expense, together with the land-tax superadded, makes the production of cotton impossible. [Brown's pamphlet.] Wool is pretty extensively grown throughout the presidency, and considerable attention is now paying to the improvement of the breeds of sheep. Indigo is also largely cultivated; and the silk-worm is becoming an object of attention. This presidency, in common with the rest of India, has the advantage of two annual harvests. Rice, Indian corn, joar—the dhurra of the Arabs-millet, and pulses, are sown about June, or at the beginning of the rainy season, and reaped at its conclusion. The spring or dry season harvest is sown in October, when the rains have ceased, and is reaped in February or March. The crops of this harvest consist of wheat, barley, and millet. Oats, beans, pease, lentiles, chick pease, and pigeon pease are also cultivated. Colonel Sykes is of opinion that India, in cases of dearth in England, could be looked to with confidence for a supply of breadstuffs; and India having the advantage of its principal crops ripening in January and February, the moment a failure of the crop should be known in England in August, orders for supplies from the following January crops of India could be sent, and the supplies landed in England, even by the route of the Cape of Good Hope, two or three months before the ripening of the crops in England. Colonel Sykes, in a paper read to the British Association, furnished averaged ALL ALL STANDARD AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AN enumerates many grains, cheap, nutritive, and in general consumption, which do not appear in the price-lists, and whose names even are unknown in Europe, except to the learned. The first price-list gives an average from 1827 to 1845, at seven markets in the Deccan under the Bombay presidency. The ultimate result is shown in the following table:- Avoird, weight of Price per quarter English. grain for 2s. lb. OZ. Iowaree, . . . . Now these 100 lbs. weight of iowaree, for 2s., are sufficient for the support of a man for two months at the least. But in 1828 and 1843, at the market of Kullus, the average price of iowaree was 204 lbs. for 2s., or more than 2 lbs. for a farthing; so that in those years a man could live for less than a farthing per diem for meat. The tobacco lands of Guzerat are said to be remarkably well-managed; and the coffee grown on the Malabar coast is of a very superior quality. Population and Education.] The estimate of the pop. already inserted represents the pop. of this presidency as having reached 6,638,810 in 1826; but this is a vague estimate, and probably wide of the truth. Colonel Sykes, in a paper on the statistics of civil and criminal justice in British India, read before the Statistical society in March 1843, estimated the pop. of this presidency at only 6,270,461. This pop., as well as that of the other presidencies, is composed of various tribes and nations, differing as much from each other, in language, manners, and physiognomy, as the French and Portuguese from the Greeks, Germans, and Poles. The number of resident Europeans in this presidency is smaller in proportion to its pop. than in the other presidencies. In 1830-31, the total number of schools within the presidency was 1,705, and the scholars attending them 35,153. Of these schools 25, attended by 1,315 scholars, were supported by the Company; and the remaining 1,680 were merely village schools. The Government institutions are under the management of a Board-ofeducation, consisting of a president, 5 members, and a secretary. In 1843, 3 of the board were Europeans, 1 a Parsi, 1 a Hindu, and 1 a Mahommedan. A maximum sum of 1,25,000 rupees is fixed for educational purposes under the presidency; and the Board have the control of a sum of 20,000 rupees constituting the Sanscrit college fund. The Elphinstone Native education institute was attended by 618 in 1841, and 587 in 1842. It has its European professors and native tutors. Provincial English schools exist at Punah, Tannah, Surat, and Panwell. The Sanscrit college was attended by 95 paying and 82 non-paying students in 1841; by 8 paying and 68 nonpaying in 1842. Government sanctions the establishment of district vernacular schools where the pop. amounts to 2,000. The masters are trained in a normal class at Punah. Their minimum monthly salary appears to be 10 rupees. The vernacular schoolbooks consist of translations into Mahrattee, Guzeratti, and Canarese, of treatises on algebra, geometry, grammar, geography, history, and moral instruction. The number of vernacular schools in 1842 was 120; of pupils 7,750. On the whole population it appears that in 1842, 17.3 was the proportion of children receiving instruction to every 100 capable of receiving it in this presidency; and that of this proportion 15 per cent. were instructed in the indigenous schools; and only 2.3 per cent. in the government schools. The church Missionary society have at Bombay 7 - Mahratta schools with 470 scholars, and 103 pupils | in a money-school; the Institution of the Church of Scotland mission, opened in July 1845, has above 200 pupils; the Free Church of Scotland mission have 20 schools, with 1,000 pupils; and the American Board of mission have 9 schools, with 395 scholars. Besides these Missionary schools in the city and island of Bombay, several others are scattered over the presidency. An hospital and college was founded at Bombay by Sir Jimsetjee Jeejeboy; and the Grant Medical college, founded by subscription, in honour of the memory of a late governor, promises to be productive of great good. A literary and a geographical society have been established at Bombay. Intimately connected with the state of education is that of the subject of criminal justice. It appears that the average number of sentences of death for the five half-years from first half of 1838 to first half of 1841, was 15.4; of transportation, 45.8; and of imprisonment for life, 7.8. The total number of convictions in 4 years by all the courts, including police cases, was 91,999, or about 23,000 annually; or 1 conviction to every 273 souls, taking the pop. of the presidency at only 6,270,461. These returns, however, did not comprise any connected with the suppression of Thugee. [Statistical Journ., vol. vi.] Government and Revenue. The government of this presidency is vested in a governor, who is subordinate to the governor-general at Calcutta, and who is assisted by three councillors. The commander-inchief is not officially a member of the council; but may be appointed by the Board of directors. The revenue in 1833-4 was £2,292,682; in 1843-4 it amounted to 3,30,55,645 rupees; in 1845-6, to 2,18,38,719 rupees. The expenditure in 1833-4 was £2,212,316; in 1843-4 it was 2,29,38,495 rupees; and in 1845-6, 2,74,12,370 rupees.—The public debt outstanding and payable in Bombay on April 30, 1845, was 41,20,31,509 rupees, or £38,627,954, bearing an annual interest of £1,823,156. Military force.] The military force maintained within this presidency in 1832 was 32,157, of whom 6,252 were Europeans. The force was distributed as follows:- In garrison at Bombay, 3,874 Northern division, Sattara subsidiary force, 759 7,016 Southern division, 6,871 Punah division, Asirgur fortress, In 1842 the total force was 32,727; and the deaths 1,070, or 3.2 per cent. per ann. In 1843, the force was 32,464; the deaths 871, or 2.7 per cent. The native troops are mostly Hindus, who are in the proportion of 11 to 1 Mussulman. The low castes constitute about & of the whole.

BOMBAY, a township of Franklin co., U. S., in the state of New York, 15 m. NE of Malone. It is generally level, and is watered by the Little Salmon and St. Regis. It contains the residue of the St. Regis Indians, who form a small settlement on the St. Lawrence; and a v., consisting of about 30 houses, inhabited by whites. Total pop. 1,446.

BOMBAY-HOOK, an island of Kent co., U. S., on the coast of the state of Delaware, formed by an inosculation of the Duck and Little Duck. It is 9 m. in length, and about 2 in breadth. Its southern extremity is 7 m. NE of Dover.
BOMBAY'S SHOAL, a ring of low rocky islets, in the Chinese sea, to the W of the island of Palawan, in N lat. 9° 30', and E long. 116° 52'.
BOMBI, a town of Lower Guinea, in the kingdom of Congo, 30 m. S of Bamba, and 15 m. NE of Ambriz, on the l. bank of the Loze.
BOMBONON POINT, the S extremity of the island of Negros, one of the Philippine group, in the Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 9° 10', and E long. 123°.
BOMBRA, a pergunnah of Hindostan, in the BOM prov. of Gundwana, 70 m. SE of Sumbhulpur. In 1818 it was dependant upon Nagpur. Its annual assessment did not then exceed 1,050 rupees.
BOM CASTELLO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, on the r. bank of the Coa, 8 m. S of Ameida.
BOM-DESPACHO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, and comarca of Rio-das-Velhas.
BOMENI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Lapland, on the l. bank of a river of the same name, 30 m. above its entrance into Motka bay, in the Arctic sea, and 45 m. WNW from Kola.
BOMER-LES-FORGES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Orme, cant. of Domfront. Pop. 1,992.
BOMERSHOVEN, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 13 m. SW of Maestricht.

BOM-FIM, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, and comarca of Santa Cruz, on the r. bank of the Corumba, 90 m. SE of Villa Boa or Goyaz, and 50 m. NNE of S. Cruz. Gold was discovered in the environs of this place in 1744; but is no longer wrought. Cattle are reared in considerable numbers in the district, which is watered by the Passa-Quatro, the Peixe, the Piracanjuba, the Antas, and the Corumba.—Also a town and district in the prov. of Rio-Janeiro, in the comarca of Ouro-Preto, in the mountainous district to the W of the river Paranpeba. Pop. 3,000. Also a v. and district in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, in the comarca of Serro-Frio. Pop. 2,000. -Also a v. in the same prov., in the comarca of Jequitonhonta, near the river Macaubas.-Also an island off the coast of Rio-de-Janeiro, in the bay of Angra-dos-Reis.
BOMIGE, or HARING'S ISLANDS, a group of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Celebes sea, off the E coast of Borneo, to the N of the peninsula of Kanniungan, in N lat. 1° 25', and E long. 118° 45'.
BOMINY. See BAMEENY.

BOMJARDIM, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, near the r. bank of the San Francisco, 70 m. WSW of Jacobina. Pop. 1,200.—Also a town and district of Brazil, on the SE confines of the prov. of Ceara, in the comarca of Crato, 78 m. S of Va. do Forte, or Ceara. It lies in a fertile valley of the Serra-dos-Cayririz-Novos. Pop. 6,000.—Also a v. in 10,917 the prov. of Bahia, on the r. bank of the San Fran- 753 cisco.

BOM-JESUS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, and comarca of Recife, near the Capibariba river.—Also a v. in the prov. of Bahia, on an island in All Saints bay.—Also a v. in the same prov., in the comarca of Rio-de-Contas, on the r. bank of the San Francisco.—Also a v. in the same prov., in the comarca of Rio-de-San-Francisco.—Also a town in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, 12 m. W of Nova- Coimbra. Pop. 1,000.—Also an island in the bay of Nitherohi, N of the city of Rio-Janeiro.
BOM-JESUS-DA-PRAIA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, on the E side of the island of Itamaraca.
BOM-JESUS-DO-RIO-PARDO, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Sapucahi, on a stream of the same name, which flows into the Rio-Pardo, an affluent of the Rio-Grande.
BOM-JESUS-DOS-PASSOS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, and district of Ventania.
BOMKINS, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Java sea, off Indramayu Point, on the N coast of Java, in S lat. 5° 49', and E long. 108° 30'.

BOMMEL, or ZALT-BOMMEL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, on the l. side of the Waal, on an island of the same name, 7 m. SW of Thiel. Pop. 3,100. The town is pretty, and was formerly of considerable importance. Its fortifications are now, however, much dilapidated; and its harbour is much obstructed by sand-banks. It still, however, possesses some commerce, and returns two deputies to the states. In 1599 this town sustained an unsuccessful siege by the Spaniards. It was taken by the French in 1672, and again in 1794 after a long and sanguinary struggle.

BOMMELER-WAARD, an island of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, formed by the Waal and Meuse. It is about 15 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth; and is very fertile, producing grain in great abundance, and luxuriant pasturage, on which large herds of cattle are fed. It is defended by the forts of St. Andries, Crêve Coeur, and Loevenstein.
BOMMEL FIORD, an inlet on the W coast of Norway, between the prov. of Stavanger, and the Bommel-Oe.
BOMMEL-OE, an island on the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 59° 38', and E long. 5° 9'. It belongs to the diocese of S Bergenhuus. Its chief town is situated on a bay near its SE extremity.
BOMMES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Langon. Pop. 828. It is celebrated for its white wine.
BOMORI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 32 m. WSW of Jeytpur.

BOMPOKA, an island of the Indian ocean, in the Nicobar group, to the E of the island of Terressa, in N lat. 8° 18', E long. 93° 21'. Its circumf. does not exceed 3 m.; but it rises in a pyramidal form to a great height. It is to a considerable extent covered with wood.
BOMRAUZPOLLAM, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 60 m. WNW of Madras.

BOMST, a town of Prussia, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the prov. and 50 m. WSW of Posen, near the l. bank of the Obra, 11 m. NE of Zullichau. Pop. in 1837, 2,125, chiefly Germans, Poles, and Jews. It contains a Catholic and a Lutheran church; and has some cloth manufactories. Hops, the produce of the locality, forms its chief article of commerce. The circle contains 33,530 inhabitants. Its extensive pasturages maintain numerous herds of cattle.

BOM-SUCCESSO, or Fanado, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, and comarca of Cerro do Frio, 108 m. WSW of Porto Seguro, and 250 m. NW of Villa-Rica. It is situated on a rising ground near the Fanado, a tributary of the Arazuahi. Cotton and the sugar-cane are cultivated in the environs; and cattle are reared in considerable numbers. The locality affords gold in small quantities, and precious stones.

BOM-SUCCESSO, a small stream of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, which rises in the Serra Creola, runs from S to N, and flows into the Paranan on the BON (CAPE), RAS IDDAH, or ADDAR, a promonl. bank. tory of N Africa, the ancient Hermæum promontorium, or Promontorium Mercurii, on the coast of Tunis, in N lat. 37° 4', E long. 11° 3'. Its N extremity rises to a great height; but, at its point of junction with the continent, it sinks so low as to render it, when sighted at the distance of 15 leagues, apparently insulated. From Cape B. the coast trends 2 leagues WSW 1/2 W to Ras-el-Ahmar, on the one side; and on the other 17 m. S by E ½ E to Ras-el-Mustapha.
BON. See BONN.
BONA, a district in the p. of Inverness, in Inverness-shire, on the E side of the Ness, between the lower end of Loch Ness and the head of Loch Dochfour. The Banatia of Ptolemy is supposed to have

BO'NA, or BONAH, [FRENCH, Bône; ARAB. Beledstood here. el-'Annáb, i.e. 'Country of Jujubes,' contracted Anaba,] 3335 a district and town of Algiers, in the 3d subdivision of the prov. of Constantine, under the French administration of Algeria. The town is situated on the coast, in N lat. 36° 52', E long. 8° 10', 137 m. SE of Constantina, 76 m. E of Philippeville, 202 m. E of Bugia, and 255 m. E of Algiers. It stands on a small bay lying between Ras-el-Ahmar, or Capo di Guardia, and Point Cigogne, half-a-mile W of the embouchure of the Seibus. The bay opens to the NE; in the rear of the town, the surface rises in a series of terraces to a height of 340 ft., from which elevation the Casbah, or citadel, commands the town and the adjacent plain to the E and W. The town is surrounded by a wall, nearly 3 m. in circuit, with square towers, and four gates; the only fortification of importance, however, in the line of wall, is Fort Cicogne. The site of the town is small and contracted, and the streets are generally narrow and crooked; but since the French took possession of the place, some decent-looking streets have been formed, which bear or bore the names of Constantine, Damrémont, Saint-Louis, Fréart, and Armandy, and one or two miniature boulevards around the Place Rovigo, in the centre of the town. The town contains two Catholic churches, a mosque, and a synagogue. The hospitals are clean and commodious; and good barracks have been built in the Casbah. To the SW of the town, on the lowest terrace of the high grounds, is the redoubt of Damrémont; and surmounting it is Fort Santons. Besides these, and the Casbah, there is Fort Genois, 3 m. to the NW, on the coast; and a battery opposite the Mouillage des Caroubiers, at the distance of 2 m. in the same direction. More to the S, on the plain, are the Lion and Caserin batteries. B. is the residence of a marechal-de-camp, and the seat of a tribunal de première instance, a justice-of-peace, and a sub-director of civil affairs. It has four schools, one of which is for Moorish children, and another for Jews. Its pop. in 1843 was 4,959; in 1847, 9,799, of whom 6,006 were Europeans, and 3,793 natives, Moors, Arabs, Kabyles, Negroes, and Barbary Jews. Upwards of 1,000 of the European part of the community are Maltese, who appear to have quite monopolized the productive industry of B. French, Italians, Spaniards, Germans, and Swiss, compose the remaining five-sixths of the European pop. "B.," says a correspondent of the Times Journal, writing under date 4th August, 1846, "has always been considered the most promising and most prosperous of the Algerian French settlements on this coast. It may be said to contain the quintessence of French town colonization in North Africa, being a veritable hot-bed, or miniature, of French ideas working together in connexion and harmony with the Moors and Arabs of Barbary. It is so small, so compact, so full, everybody running one over the other, and everybody being mixed and living together in such pleasant and glorious fusion, that the French philosophy of the amalgamation of the races in N Africa has no further conquest to make on this score. With the level of races morals of course find their level; and I am of the opinion of the editor of the Afrique, that, in general, instead of the natives and the fugitive foreigners being brought up to the French standard, the French descend to the amount of morality possessed by their new allies, the Moors. But we must hope for better times, and put faith in 'progress.' Besides the advantages of security and tranquillity-the first and the most essential-B. has also some, and many, for direct and immediate colonization. I shall mention the present prospects, or elements of prosperity, of this district, considered the most prosperous of all the Algerine settlements; and so much so, that the Seybouse journal of B. asserts that capitalists prefer this district to Algiers or Oran, as the most promising and secure field for the investment of their money. The country around, at the present time, -as it had done for years past, until the late expedition of General Raudon to Tabossa, on the frontiers of Tunis, -enjoys the greatest tranquillity. The immediate environs are cultivated, and several farms have been established, but principally for the growth of tobacco, fruits and vegetables, and hay; the cultivation of grain being left mostly to the Arabs. About a year ago some iron-mines were discovered, only distant 5 m. SW of B., at a place called Bou Haniza. A certain Marquis de Bassano, with a company at his back, has purchased the working of these mines, which are said to contain an abundance of good ore, and he is now erecting furnaces for the smelting of the ore, some of which is to be conveyed to France, and the rest to be consumed here. It is said this mining enterprise promises the most brilliant success. There is also another source of prosperity in the future, that of the forests in the neighbourhood, which forthwith are to be made available. Amongst these trees is a great deal of dwarf oak; specimens of these forests have been sent to Toulon, and the ship-builders have reported that the wood is of good quality and suited to naval constructions. There is, lastly, a considerable trade said to be available with the Arabs of the district, and principally in wool; also bees'-wax, coral, wheat, barley, tobacco, olive-oil, &c., are said to be objects of commerce. No villages have yet been planted in the neighbourhood of B., but there is an encampment of troops, a fondouk (inn) for the Arabs, and a market established at a place called Ghelma, about 20 leagues in the interior; all which are much vaunted of: but to know the exact truth it would be necessary to go there. On the W side of the gulf there are placed, on the heights, two or three lights for the shipping, the coast being sufficiently dangerous. The harbour is a very poor one, and quite unprotected from NE winds. In fact, the gulf of B. is extremely exposed. At this time, being the best season, there are some 50 merchant-brigs in port, besides smaller craft, the former being employed chiefly in carrying hay from this to Algiers and Oran, and nearly all those vessels, large and small, are employed in bringing provisions and stores for the inhabitants and the garrison. In the face or on the strength of these facts and circumstances,—a densely populated town, several considerable sources of agricultural and other prosperity, and the tranquillity of the country,-I have made," says this correspondent of the Times, "frequent and general inquiries, if the colony were prosperous; or if not, was it likely to be so in future? But I found, to my surprise, a general depression in the minds of shopkeepers and men of business. They complained of the establishment of Philippeville, which, without being a prosperous place itself, had diverted their trade with Constantina from them. They complained that there was no enterprise, no confidence in mercantile transactions, no money, no capitalists; that they were all living from hand to mouth, and one upon the other, having nothing to import or export which could employ and remunerate capital to any great amount. You are undoubtedly surprised at the great number of troops in proportion to the civilians: only conceive one able-bodied soldier to two of the inhabitants, be they children or adults! However, here is the secret of the prosperity of B. Remove the troops, or even half of them, and the townspeople will be plunged into the deepest misery and distress. But everywhere in Algeria, in proportion to the number of the military, so increase the civilians, and so increases the prosperity of the settlements. Almost every second, certainly every third, house in B. is a café, or a grog-shop, and of course principally for the military. With respect to the natives, I'm puzzled BON to know how they live. The poorer men are employed as servants and porters; the more respectable keep pipe and tobacco-shops. They mostly occupy the back and narrow streets, and get out of the way of the French as much as possible. I believe they sell and trade mostly with the Arabs or country people. As a general rule, you will find the indifferent demoralized, and the disaffected the most respectable of the native populations." A steamer sails between B. and Algiers 3 times in the month; and the steamer from Marseilles to Tunis afterwards touches at B. The port of B. also communicates with Toulon, Port- Vendre, and Cette. Off B. there is only anchorage for small vessels. The Seibus affords good shelter to all vessels which can pass over its bar, which has only 4 ft. water on it. Men-of-war anchor below Fort Genois. The plain of B., of which the superficies amounts to 110,000 hectares, or 271,825 acres, is bounded on the N and E by ramifications of Jebel Idugh, or Mont Edough; on the S by the Bujimah river, running from SW to NE; and on the W by the hills of Msour. The Seibus, a larger river, runs in a course nearly parallel to the Bujimah; and the plain is watered by several canals led from these two streams in various directions. In the vicinity of Fort Genois is a quarry of white marble, tinged with blue and grey. Pink marble forms the Capo di Guardia. Cipollino is said to exist in Jebel Idugh. About 12 m. to the SW of B. is Lake Fezzara, 10 m. in length, and 64 m. in breadth. It abounds with wild fowl, and its shores give shelter to wild boars. The road to Constantina is the principal route leading from B. It leads by the fortified camp of El Dra'an and Guelma; and sends off a branch to La Calle, and another to Philippeville. The arrond. of Bona is divided into the 4 circles of B., La Calle on the coast, Guelma on the Seibris, and l'Edough.—The ruins of the ancient Hippona are spread over the neck of land lying between the Seibus and the Bujimah. They are about 1 m. in circuit. History.] The town of B. was founded by the Arabs in 697, and was built of materials taken from the ruins of Hippona, or Hippo Regius, 31 m. to the NW. The Tunisians, who long regarded B. as marking the extreme limit of their territory in this direction, built the Casbah in 1300. The Genoese established a coral fishery on the coast of B. In 1535, Andrew Doria, in pursuit of Barbarussa, entered B. and found it deserted. In August 1830, the French general Damrémont made a descent upon this coast, but was recalled before he had achieved the conquest of B. Ibrahim, ex-bey of Constantina, retired here; but was followed by the French troops who got possession of the Casbah and town on the 26th of March, 1832.

BONACCA, or GUANAJA, an island of the Caribbean sea, in Honduras bay, in N lat. 16° 27', and W long. 87° 38'. It is 9 m. in length from NE to SW, and from 1 to 3 m. in breadth. Near the centre it attains an alt. of 1,500 ft. It is covered with wood, and uninhabited except during the turtling season from April to August.
BONACHA DE ALARCON. See ALARCON.
BONADUTZ, or Bonaduz, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, near the confluence of the Upper Rhine with the Lower Rhine, opposite Reichenau, and 8 m. WSW of Chur. Pop. 685.
BONAGA, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom of Borgu, 5 m. E of Kiama. It was visited by Clapperton in 1826.

BONAIRE, or BUEN AYRE, an island in the Caribbean sea, in the group and 33 m. E of Curaçoa. Its NE point is in N lat. 12° 14', W long. 68° 181'. It is 22 m. in length from NW to SE; and 4 m. in breadth. Pop. in 1833, 1,348, of whom 112 were whites, 1,069 people of colour, and 167 slaves. It affords excellent timber, and has extensive salt and lime-works. The lime is made from coral stone. Potatoes and various leguminous herbs are extensively cultivated for the supply of Curaçoa. Asses and goats are reared in great numbers; and the adjacent sea abounds with fish. The town of B. has a good port, and is the residence of a sub-governor.

BONALLY, a hamlet 5 m. SW of Edinburgh, and 1½ m. S of Colinton, on the N declivity of the Pentland hills. Professor Forbes ascertained the mean temp. here during the years 1832, to be 44° 27' at 8 a. m. and 44° 14' at 8 p. m.
BONAMAES, or Bonamos, a town of the duchy of Nassau, on the r. bank of the Nidda, 4 m. SSE of Homburg, and 5 m. NNW of Frankfurt. It has a Lutheran church. Pop. 428.

BONANZA, a small town and port of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 43 m. SSW of Seville, and 20 m. N of Cadiz, on the l. side of the estuary of the Guadalquiver, 1 m. within its mouth. It possesses a castle; and is now the out-port of Seville. The district between B. and San Lucar at the mouth of the river is a dreary, desert-like, sandy waste.
BONAPARTE (ISLE). See BOURBON.
BONAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. NNE of Leon, on the l. bank of the Porma. In the vicinity are some thermal springs.

BONAR, a hamlet in Sutherlandshire, in the p. of Criech, 12 m. W of Dornoch. The Great Highland road is here carried across the Dornoch frith, at a point where it is contracted to a breadth of 70 yards at low water, by a bridge of which the central arch, of 150 ft. span, is of iron, and two other arches of stone are 60 and 50 ft. respectively.

BONARCADO, a town of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. of Cagliari, 8 m. NNE of Tristano, on the Milis. Pop. 897. The environs contain mines of copper, lead, and iron. BONASH, Bunass, or Manas, a large but almost unknown river of India, which takes its rise in the mountains on the N frontier of Bhotan, runs WSW, and entering Lower Assam, unites with the Brahmaputra, 8 m. NNW of Goyalpara.
BONAT, a town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, 11 m. NNE of Gueret, on a hill, near the r. bank of the Little Creuse. Pop. 2,050. It contains the old castle of Beauvais, and some Roman remains.
BONATI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of principato Citra, 3 m. ENE of Policastro. Pop. 3,300.
BONAVENTURA, a river of Lower Canada, which rises in Cox township, and, crossing the SE of Hamilton township, falls into the bay of Chaleurs. Its estuary forms an excellent and commodious harbour.
BONAVENTURA. See BUONAVENTURA.

BONAVENTURE, an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, about 14 m. E from the Percé rock, on the E coast of the peninsula of Gaspe, in N lat. 48° 30', and W long. 10° 18.' It is a barren rock, and a few fishermen are its only inhabitants.
BONAVENTURE HEAD, a headland on the SE coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 48° 16½, W long. 53° 18′.
BONA VISTA. See BOA VISTA.

BONA VISTA BAY, an extensive indentation on the NE coast of the island of Newfoundland, between Cape Freel on the N, and Cape Bona Vista on the S, in N lat. 49°, and W long. 53° 30'. Its entrance is 39 m. in breadth, but its area is much impeded by the numerous islets with which it is studded, and its whole coast is rocky and dangerous.

BONA VISTA (CAPE), the E extremity of Random island, and at the SE point of the bay of the same name, on the coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 48° 42′, and W long. 52° 59′. There is a lighthouse on this cape, with a revolving light, at an elevation of 150 ft. above sea-level. It was discovered by Cabot in 1478.
BONA VISTA, or GULL ISLAND, an islet off the BON coast of Newfoundland, half-a-mile NNE of Cape Bona Vista.
BONAVY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nord, com. of Banteux. Pop. 298.

BONAWASI, or BANAWASSEE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, in the dist. and 18 m. SE of Sunda, on the l. bank of the Vorda. It formerly consisted of about 500 houses, but is now greatly diminished in size. It is mentioned by Ptolemy, and is of great antiquity.
BONBOILLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Marney. Pop. 298.
BONBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 6 m. N of Glandford Bridge. Area 2,900 acres. Pop. 386.
